summary: Kenneth Suna explains why he avoids selling cash-secured puts using HIMS stock as an example. He demonstrates that while a $54 strike put on HIMS offers $115 in premium, the inability to exercise early and the obligation to wait until Friday expiration creates opportunity cost—missing chances to buy at better prices mid-week if the stock rebounds. He prefers keeping cash available for flexible entry points rather than locking in small premiums.
takeaways: Cash-secured puts cannot be exercised early; must wait until Friday expiration date
$115 premium on HIMS $54 put strike requires $5,400 cash tied up
Opportunity cost example: stock drops to $52 Wednesday but rebounds to $57 Friday—miss better entry point while earning only $115
Use Fibonacci retracements to identify potential pullback targets ($54 level on HIMS)
Keep cash available in account for flexible buying power at optimal prices throughout the week, not locked into Friday close

strategy_candidates.change: Consider selling call spreads or covered calls instead of cash-secured puts to capture premium while maintaining capital flexibility
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly states he'd rather keep $5,400 cash free to buy at any point during the week at prices he chooses, rather than earn $115 premium and be locked into Friday expiration. This suggests Brian's Premium Desk should weight covered calls (which allow early assignment and capital release) more heavily than puts in the wheel strategy when expecting pullbacks.

title: Another Reason I don't ALWAYS Like Cash Secured Puts
